planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; do not fertilize — rich soils produce floppy plants. The milky sap may irritate skin.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, average to well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; blue star is a native perennial that earns its keep in three seasons — in late spring with starry pale blue flowers on arching stems, in summer with clean willow-like foliage that remains attractive and disease-free all season, and most dramatically in autumn with spectacular golden-yellow fall color that rivals the finest maples. It is one of the most structural and elegant native perennials available, naturally found in moist woodland edges and stream banks. It tolerates light to moderate shade well. The deer-resistant foliage contains a milky latex. Do not over-fertilize; lean conditions produce compact, self-supporting plants. Cut back lightly after bloom to maintain tidy habit.

planting.note: Plant container vines in spring near a structure, tree, or fence; the vine clings by adhesive suction-cup pads that do not penetrate surfaces or damage mortar. Do not confuse with poison ivy (three leaflets); virginia creeper has five.

directions: Plant in virtually any soil — wet or dry, rich or poor, sandy or clay — in full sun to deep shade; Virginia creeper is one of the most versatile native vines, blanketing fences, walls, trees, and arbors in deep shade where few other plants grow, and producing a stunning curtain of brilliant crimson-scarlet-burgundy fall color that is among the most vivid of any plant in the North American flora. The small dark blue-black berries that follow in fall are an essential food for migrating thrushes, waxwings, bluebirds, and woodpeckers. It clings by adhesive pads (not invasive roots), making it appropriate on stone and brick. The sap and berries are toxic to humans and can cause contact dermatitis in sensitive individuals; the similar five-leaflet compound leaf distinguishes it from three-leaflet poison ivy. Prune aggressively in late winter to control size.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs or rooted suckers in spring in any soil in partial to full shade; coralberry tolerates dry shade better than almost any other native shrub, making it useful under mature trees where most plants fail.

directions: Plant in any well-drained soil — including dry, rocky, and infertile — in partial to full shade; coralberry is one of the most shade-tolerant and drought-tolerant of all native shrubs, growing naturally on dry limestone bluffs, rocky woodland slopes, and under dense tree canopy where little else survives. The small pinkish flowers in early summer attract native bees; the small coral-pink to deep red berry-like drupes that follow in fall persist into winter and provide food for bobwhite, wild turkey, mockingbirds, and other birds. The plant spreads by root suckers to form dense, erosion-controlling colonies — excellent for dry, difficult slopes under trees. The cultivar 'Hancock' is particularly dense and ground-hugging. All parts mildly toxic if ingested in quantity.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; this plant establishes quickly and spreads by rhizome to form a dense colony. Tolerates periodic flooding.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained to moist soil in partial to full shade; yellowroot is a low, colony-forming native shrub of Appalachian stream banks and moist woodland slopes, grown for its ferny, compound foliage that provides attractive, fine-textured groundcover in shade, its drooping panicles of small maroon-purple flowers in early spring, and its outstanding orange-gold fall color. The roots and inner bark are vividly yellow from the alkaloid berberine (also found in goldenseal), historically used as a natural yellow dye and for medicinal purposes. As a groundcover, it spreads vigorously by rhizome to form weed-suppressing mats under trees, on moist slopes, and along shaded stream banks. One of the best native low-maintenance groundcovers for moist shade.

planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in early spring or late fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; transplants well from container stock but resents root disturbance once established.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; American hornbeam (also called musclewood or blue beech) is one of the finest small native understory trees, growing naturally in the deep shade of bottomland forests and stream banks across eastern North America. The distinctive smooth, gray, sinewy bark with ridge-like muscle-like sinews makes it an outstanding specimen year-round. Delicate hop-like seed clusters in fall provide food for many finch and songbird species. Outstanding golden-orange to orange-red fall color. It grows slowly — 10–12 inches per year — but is very long lived. One of the few small trees genuinely suited to planting in deep shade under large oaks or maples. Excellent for naturalistic woodland gardens and native plant landscapes.
